Correction and management of dermal filler misplacement post lip augmentation
abstract: This paper outlines a case study of a 30-year-old woman who presented to the author, an aesthetic nurse consultant, with concerns about her lip after having a dermal filler injected a few weeks before. During her consultation, it was discovered that she was initially seen by another aesthetic nurse practitioner in the US, who injected a hyaluronic acid (HA) based dermal filler for the purpose of lip volume augmentation. This case study will reflect on how the patient was assessed and treated, considering accountability, legal and ethical issues, as well as concordance with the medical advice given to the patient. The author will also discuss diagnosis and differential diagnosis, and describe the best available treatment options that were sought to resolve the patient's condition.

title: Effects of radiation exposure on dermal collagen: A multi modal approach
abstract: Intense pulsed light (IPL) is a high-intensity treatment for skin disorders and ageing. As this treatment regime is often poorly regulated and inadequately studied, we investigate IPL as a cosmetic device and its effects on dermal collagen components of the skin. Biopsies from the back-neck folds of a 4-week-old, 25 kg large white pig were irradiated with intense pulsed light (IPL) (l= 584 nm) at an increased radiation dose of 40 J/cm<sup>2</sup> once, thrice and ten times. Samples were cryo-sectioned (10 μm) and stained with picro sirrus red. Ex-vivo biopsies were assessed with polarized light microscopy (PLM), atomic force microscopy (AFM) and scanning acoustic microscopy. Customized software was used to map the sound speed and attenuation on the ultrasonic images Differences in collagen structure were observed between all three levels of irradiation progressing depth-wise into the epidermis. Ex-vivo porcine tissue demonstrated loss of D-banding and gelatinization with increasing dermal depth with higher intensities. Acoustic microscopy demonstrated a significant decrease in sound speed and attenuation that relates to the number of exposures. Sound speed decreases at much faster rates than attenuation.

title: Evidence-based management of melasma: contributing factors and treatment options
abstract: Melasma, a skin condition of adults in which light to dark brown or greyish patches of pigmentation develop mainly on facial skin, is one of the most common pigmentary disorders seen by health professionals. The condition is more common in women, especially those with darker skin types, than in men; however, one in four men with darker skin types can be affected. Treating melasma poses challenges for health professionals due to its multifactorial pathology. Even though melasma is a widely recognised cause of significant psychological distress, there is a lack of systematic and clinically usable treatment algorithms and guidelines for melasma management. This article explores the causes of epidemiology of melasma and explains recommended treatments. Aesthetic practitioners are well placed to offer patient education and provide corrective treatments, including topical solutions, sun protection, chemical peels and laser procedures.

title: Laser skin rejuvenation: common complications and risk management
abstract: Cosmetic interventions are continually developing to help consumers find risk-free solutions to their appearance woes. Although laser skin resurfacing has been a successful treatment of this kind, the risks associated with any procedure should be investigated fully. Naomi Di-Scala outlines the types of laser skin resurfacing and discusses the potential side effects for all practitioners to be aware of

title: Managing dermal filler complications part 2: nodules and granulomas
abstract: Injectable soft tissue fillers have become an increasingly popular option for patients seeking facial rejuvenation. Their rise in popularity has been driven by a number of factors, most notably their minimally invasive nature. However, despite this, it is important for aesthetic practitioners to be aware that various complications can still occur. Fortunately, the majority of dermal filler adverse events are minor and transient, although the patient may consider them aesthetically displeasing and unacceptable. Major complications are rare, but can produce permanent, disfiguring damage. This article explores the development of nodules and granulomas following the administration of dermal fillers and outlines several treatment options, with the goal of helping practitioners to better understand the aetiology of inflammatory nodules and tailor their treatment strategies for this complication.

title: Mesotherapy for skin rejuvenation: a brief and practical insight for aesthetic practitioners
abstract: Smooth and even skin possesses a form of magnetism, and people understand it as the external manifestation of a healthy lifestyle. New studies reveal the efficacy of mesotherapy in reducing external signs of ageing, such as wrinkles, large pores, sun damage and loss of glow, elasticity and firmness. The capacity of mesotherapy to stimulate and repair the mechanisms of the skin, creating a suitable environment for the fibroblasts to produce elastin and collagen, offers an effective long-lasting answer to most age-related skin concerns. The full practical aspects needed to perform mesotherapy treatments must be covered in training sessions in person, as the accuracy of mesotherapy depends on many considerations. To practice mesotherapy, practitioners need to have a deep theoretical understanding of the ageing process and of the techniques and materials that are used in mesotherapy.

title: Practice standard for the management of dermal filler complications
abstract: In recent years, a great deal of attention has been paid to the diagnosis and management of complications arising from the use of dermal fillers. However, alongside the clinical factors, professional considerations must not be neglected, because to do so can lead to often avoidable claims

title: Profhilo Structura for skin rejuvenation
abstract: Profhilo Structura, a skin treatment by IBSA Derma, uses stabilised hybrid cooperative complexes (HCCs) of high and low molecular weight hyaluronic acid (HA) to enhance skin hydration, firmness and texture. This case study examines its effects on a 54-year-old female with sun damage, dyschromia, and lower face skin laxity. Treatment plan: two sessions of Profhilo Structura were performed, spaced four weeks apart, targeting skin laxity and sagging in the lower face. The patient, classified as a ‘sagger’, sought to improve firmness without dermal filler use. Methods: a 22-gauge cannula was used to administer the product along the zygomatic arch and pre-auricular area. The treatment was minimally uncomfortable, rated at 2/10 (NRS).

title: Using cannulas for dermal filler placement: why, when and how?
abstract: Traditionally, dermal fillers have been administered to the face using a hypodermic needle. However, injecting using cannulas has become increasingly popular over the past decade, due to the changing goals of treatment, aims to improve patient experience and attemting to prevent complications. Many dermal filler products are now sold packaged with both a needle and a cannula in order to give the practitioner the choice of injection tool. It is therefore essential that the aesthetic practitioner is well educated on the topic of how to select the best injection tool for a procedure. This article aims to asses why we have seen an increase in the use of cannulas in administering this procedure, with the hope of helping the aesthetic practitioner to make the best decision regarding injection tools.

title: When can acyclovir be prescribed when treating the lips and oral commissures?
abstract: Herpes simplex virus affects billions of people around the world, and its activation can be triggered by the injection of dermal fillers in and around the lips. Amanda Hewlett looks at whether the aesthetic nurse practioner should be prophylactically prescribing acyclovir to patients with a history of herpes simplex who are considering augmentation in this area

registry_journal: Journal of Clinical and Cosmetic Dermatology
title: Keloid Excision with Debulking Technique on the Lobule Auricle Sinistra
abstract: Introduction: Keloids are fibroproliferative lesions caused by abnormal wound healing and are characterized by excessive collagen deposits that cannot heal spontaneously. Keloid in the ear is a challenging condition to be treated by doctors, especially the large ones. This condition has a psychosocial impact on the patient and a consideration for appearance is often the main reason for patients seeking to cure keloids, although there is a high possibility of keloid recurrence. Out of various keloid therapy options, one method that can be done is surgical excision therapy with debulking technique. Case report: A case of keloids in left ear lobule measuring 2.5 cm × 3 cm is reported after the patient does ear piercing with complaints of slight itch and aesthetical disturbance. Keloids enlarge 6 months after piercing. The case is treated by debulking excision surgery using local anesthesia Pehacain® (lidocaine 2% + adrenaline 1:80,000). At the end of the wound closure, 40 mg of triamcinolone acetone is given. It is regiven one week after the stitches are removed and repeatedly every 2 weeks for 4 times. Excision surgical results are good and the patient feels satisfied. Conclusion: Excision surgery with debulking technique is chosen because of the large size of the lesion and to ensure there is no residual keloid tissue at the site of predilection. Corticosteroid injection is given immediately after excision to decrease fibroblast proliferation, collagen and glycosaminoglycan synthesis and suppress proinflammatory mediators in order to prevent keloid recurrence.

registry_journal: Journal of Clinical and Cosmetic Dermatology
title: Ultrasound-Guided Injection of Hyaluronic Gel Filler in the Temples: A Report of Two Cases
abstract: Introduction: The aim of this case report was to assess the effect of injecting hyaluronic acid gel filler in the temporal fossa for the correction of skeletonisation in this specific anatomical area of the ageing face.

Facial enhancement starts with skin rejuvenation and maintenance
abstract: Today's aesthetic clinics feature a vast range of non-surgical facial aesthetic procedures on their treatment menus. From laser and light modalities to chemical peels and platelet-rich plasma, there are several options for practitioners to endorse to improve the appearance of their patients' skin. Victoria Manning highlights the role of rejuvenating skin treatments in facial enhancement

title: Using platelet-rich plasma for skin rejuvenation
abstract: The skin is the largest organ of the body, and it is affected by both intrinsic and extrinsic ageing factors. Platelet-rich plasma is an effective treatment for skin ageing, whether as a standalone treatment or in combination with other treatment modalities. The results depend on the skin quality of the patient, compliance and aftercare. Other factors to consider include the platelet-rich plasma kits and centrifuge used by the practitioner, as well as whether it is activated or not. In this article, Claudia McGloin will discuss skin ageing and the use of platelet-rich plasma for skin rejuvenation.

title: Beaches and botulinum toxin in Spain, but no aesthetic nurses ‘as such’
abstract: With both surgical and non-surgical cosmetic procedures on the rise worldwide, aesthetic nursing is becoming an increasingly popular profession. In this new series, Aysha Mendes examines aesthetic nursing practices and cultural perceptions of beauty across the globe by focusing on a different country every month. This issue focuses on medical aesthetic procedures and regulation in Spain

title: Bleach peeling for melasma: an effective approach to treatment
abstract: Melasma is rare before puberty and most commonly occurs in women during their reproductive years. It not only has a notable effect on the appearance of a patient, but also it has a significant impact on his or her psychological wellbeing. Safe and effective treatment of melasma remains a substantial challenge, and it is difficult to find a way to manage the condition in the long term as it tends to reoccur. This article aims to give a comprehensive overview of the treating principles for melasma using tailored topical bleach peel protocols. The article will discuss relevant topical depigmeting agents that can be applied to effectively treat and manage melasma. Treatment protocols are based on more than 20 years of expertise and experience of a dermatologist specialised in treating this skin disease.

title: Body contouring: a sculptural art
abstract: Lucy Glancey discusses the ways in which our aesthetic preferences for the shape of the human body can change over time, and the methods aesthetic practitioners are using to keep up with these ever-evolving preferences

title: Facial rejuvenation and skin-tightening using monopolar radiofrequency
abstract: This article will discuss how the use of monopolar radiofrequency for skin tightening is increasing due to the popular demand of non-surgical treatments with minimum downtime. It will show how to carry out the correct consultation and assessment and what considerations to take into account, as well as explaining how intrinsic and extrinsic factors contribute to ageing and the different types of radiofrequency available on the market. The use of radiofrequency in conjunction with other anti-ageing collagen-stimulating treatments to support and optimise final treatment results will also be detailed. The focus will be on the technology and treatment used with the BTL Exilis Ultra 360™ device and how it compares to other skin-tightening treatments.

title: Focusing on chin augmentation: treating the lower face using dermal filler
abstract: Aesthetic treatments to enhance body parts, such as the lips, cheek and jaw, are on the rise, and chin augmentation in particular is a popular choice. Alex Henderson details the process behind lower face augmention and considerations that should be made

title: Future of dermal fillers: moves forward for regulation and quality assurance
abstract: The question of whether dermal fillers should be reclassified as prescription-only medicines has been prominent in the aesthetics industry for many years, but it is only now that the Department of Health is paying attention to the regulation of cosmetic interventions. In this interview with Sally Taber, Journal of Aesthetic Nursing editor Natasha Devan finds out what the future of these medical devices is likely to be

title: Internal factors contributing to optimal skin health and rejuvenation
abstract: Health professionals specialising in aesthetic medicine are continually learning about new skin products and treatments they can use to help their patients look the best they can. However, aesthetic practitioners must not forget the significance of internal factors pertinent to skin rejuvenation, such as diet and hormone balance, which contribute to the health of the skin and can affect the results patients can achieve. There are a few important internal factors that, with a little education and implementation, can be easily incorporated into patients' lifestyles to achieve long-term benefits—not just for their skin, but also for their health and wellbeing. In this article, the author explores the common internal factors that affect skin health, such as gut health, nutrition, hormonal balance and emotional wellbeing. The author also recommends some additional tests and supplements that practitioners can offer to patients who wish to address internal factors in their treatment plan.

title: Non-invasive body contouring treatments: should they be embraced by aesthetic nurses?
abstract: Although liposuction was initially the most commonly performed procedure for patients who want to tackle unwanted fat and re-shape their bodies, male and female patients are now looking for time and cost-effective treatments with fewer associated risks. In this debate article, aesthetic nurse and clinical trainer Tricia Misener asks whether nurses should be adding this procedure to their treatment menus

title: Not just needles: alternative approaches to skin rejuvenation
abstract: Dermal fillers and botulinum toxin A are among the most popular skin rejuvenation treatments worldwide, and it is therefore understandable that many aesthetic practitioners choose to focus their attention on providing these treatments. However, there are several other options when it comes to rejuvenating the skin that are sometimes overlooked. In this article, Olivia Kunde explores some of these alternative approaches to skin rejuvenation, and explains why these treatments are an asset to an aesthetic practitioner's armamentarium

title: Parliamentary reception on the future of dermal fillers in the UK
abstract: Following the highly anticipated publication of Sir Keogh's final recommendations for the regulation of cosmetic interventions, practitioners are now waiting with baited breath for the Department of Health's implementation plan. To find out the latest information on the future of dermal fillers in the UK, Natasha Devan, editor of the Journal of Aesthetic Nursing, attended a seminar at the House of Lords

title: Platelet-rich plasma therapy: from facial rejuvenation to bone regeneration
abstract: Fibroblast proliferation and collagen production are key to facial rejuvenation. Just as topical growth factors are used to rejuvenate photodamaged skin and increase collagen production ( Sundaram et al, 2009 ), the administration of a solution high in growth factor proteins into the dermis or subdermis may prove a valuable tool both in the regeneration of facial tissues and the treatment of other common aesthetic indications (e.g. stretch marks and scars). Platelet-rich plasma therapy acts as a scaffold in vivo and allows aesthetic practitioners to administer a high concentration of growth factor proteins into various tissue types to stimulate fibroblast proliferation and collagen synthesis.

title: Postpartum skin conditions: managing melasma gravidarum in cosmetic practice
abstract: Melasma gravidarum is just one specific dermatoses related to pregnancy or after ingestion of oral contraceptives that is often misunderstood in practice. Given that it affects 75% of women it is highly likely that aesthetic nurses will come into contact with this at some point in their career. This article looks at the treatment options available from topical agents, chemoexfoliation and laser light therapy with an explanation of contraindications and unpredictable results, which can leave the patient and practitioner frustrated. This article clearly identifies the important role aesthetic nurses play in assessing, implementing, managing and evaluating treatment for their patients with melasma gravidarum. Furthermore, it demonstrates that advances in technology are yet to provide the aesthetic industry with an evidence-based cure.

title: Radiofrequency microneedling in aesthetic practice
abstract: Dr Paul Charlson discusses the use of radiofrequency microneedling for skin rejuvenation and addressing skin concerns

title: The voice of aesthetic nursing hit the nationals, but the true message was lost
abstract: When the national press published a news story based on a paper discussing injecting young people with botulinum toxin in the Journal of Aesthetic Nursing, the whole world took note. Helena Collier discusses how the coverage of her paper advanced the profession of aesthetic nursing in the UK and beyond, and highlights the real clinical message she wanted to get across to practitioners

title: Treatment of multiple skin concerns with an alpha hydroxy acid superficial skin peel
abstract: Alpha hydroxy acids (AHAs) are a class of chemicals that have been used in cosmeceuticals for many years. They are well known for their ability to rejuvenate the skin and their anti-ageing, pigmentation-reducing and volume-restoring properties. The PCA Skin Perfecting Peel (Church Pharmacy) contains 20% mandelic acid and 10% lactic acid. It is a superficial peel formulated to treat a variety of skin concerns, including hyperpigmentation, acne and common signs of ageing. This article looks at the evidence available to support the key ingredients in the PCA Skin Perfecting Peel, as well as its indications and contraindications. It also presents three case studies with results obtained following a course of just two peels and an at-home regimen. The author concludes this peel is an asset for any aesthetic practice, with just one treatment producing results for a variety of skin concerns, affecting a broad range of patients. The nature of chemical peels means that patients often return more frequently than with injectable therapies, which can provide ongoing contact and regular income.

title: Microfocused ultrasound : aesthetic medicine - ultrasound
abstract: Every so often in the aesthetic medical field a new device, a new technology comes along which, for all practical purposes, changes the paradigm of how we practice aesthetic medicine. Such is the case with the microfocused high-intensity fractional ultrasound system (HIFU), which has changed the way many of us approach and treat patients with skin laxity.

title: New Hyaluronic Acid Filler for Subdermal and Long-lasting Volume Restoration of the Face
abstract: Over the past few years, the focus in the field of aesthetic medicine has gradually shifted towards minimally invasive procedures. As more and more patients are refusing to take prolonged absences from their work, the use of filler materials, botulinum toxin A, peelings, laser, fat implantation and other minor surgical corrections has significantly increased. Minimally invasive volume restoration of the face through long-lasting resorbable fillers is nowadays more attractive and important to patients.
